q: How do AI, ML, deep learning, generative AI, and agentic AI relate to each other — and what is the key distinction between each layer?
a: These five terms form a nested hierarchy. AI is the broadest discipline: building systems that perform tasks requiring human-like intelligence. ML is a subset of AI where systems learn patterns from data rather than following hand-coded rules — algorithms like random forests, gradient boosting, and linear regression are classic ML. Deep learning is a subset of ML that uses multi-layer neural networks to learn hierarchical representations, enabling breakthroughs in images, speech, and text that shallower ML could not achieve. Generative AI is a class of deep-learning models (transformers, diffusion models) trained to produce new content — text, images, code, audio — rather than just classify or predict. Agentic AI sits at the application layer above all of these: it orchestrates one or more AI models to plan multi-step tasks, call tools, take actions in the world, and iterate toward a goal autonomously without requiring a human prompt at each step. The conceptual ladder is: AI ⊇ ML ⊇ Deep Learning ⊇ GenAI; Agentic AI uses GenAI as its reasoning engine but adds memory, tool-use, and autonomous goal pursuit.
fj: Generative AI and agentic AI are the same thing — both generate content and can act autonomously.
fs: GenAI refers specifically to models that produce new content (text, images). Agentic AI is an architectural pattern that uses GenAI as a reasoning core but adds planning, tool invocation, and multi-step autonomous execution — a chatbot is GenAI, an agent that books a flight end-to-end is agentic AI.

q: You are explaining the AI/ML family tree to a business stakeholder. How would you succinctly differentiate traditional ML from deep learning and GenAI using a concrete example for each?
a: Traditional ML learns structured patterns from tabular data using statistical algorithms. Example: a gradient-boosted model trained on transaction features (amount, merchant, time) to flag fraud — every feature must be hand-engineered. Deep learning removes most feature engineering by using neural networks with many layers that automatically learn representations from raw data. Example: a convolutional neural network that detects tumors directly from MRI pixel data without manual feature extraction. Generative AI extends deep learning with the goal of producing new content: a large language model trained on billions of text tokens learns to generate coherent paragraphs, summarise documents, or write code. Example: asking Amazon Bedrock's Claude to draft a product description. The key practical distinctions are: ML needs clean tabular features and is highly interpretable; deep learning needs large raw-data sets and GPU compute; GenAI needs enormous pre-training corpora and excels at open-ended content creation. On AWS, SageMaker Autopilot covers classic ML, SageMaker JumpStart offers pre-trained deep learning models, and Amazon Bedrock serves GenAI foundation models.
fj: Deep learning always outperforms traditional ML, so there is no reason to use classic algorithms like XGBoost on AWS.
fs: For structured tabular data (most business datasets), gradient-boosted trees often outperform deep learning with far less compute and data. AWS recommends XGBoost via SageMaker for many tabular tasks precisely because of its efficiency and interpretability. Deep learning's advantage emerges with unstructured data at scale.

q: Describe the four SageMaker inference modes — real-time, batch transform, asynchronous, and serverless — and explain when you would choose each.
a: SageMaker provides four inference deployment options. Real-time inference deploys a persistent endpoint on always-on instances; it handles requests within milliseconds (payload ≤ 6 MB, processing ≤ 60 s) and is ideal for user-facing APIs where latency is paramount, though you pay for idle time. Batch transform spins up temporary compute, runs predictions over an entire S3 dataset, writes results back to S3, then terminates; it suits offline scoring of large datasets (GBs to TBs) where latency is irrelevant and cost efficiency matters. Asynchronous inference queues long-running or large-payload requests (up to 1 GB, up to 1 hour processing) and can scale instances to zero when idle, making it cost-effective for bursty heavy workloads like document processing or video analysis. Serverless inference provisions compute on-demand per invocation with no instance management (payload ≤ 4 MB, ≤ 60 s); you pay only for actual compute used and it handles intermittent or unpredictable traffic, but cold starts add latency. Decision heuristic: user-facing low-latency → real-time; nightly large dataset → batch; large payloads with queuing tolerance → async; spiky lightweight traffic → serverless.
fj: Serverless inference is always cheaper than real-time inference because you only pay per request.
fs: Serverless is cheaper for intermittent, low-volume traffic. Under sustained high-throughput load, real-time endpoints on provisioned instances become more cost-effective because serverless incurs per-invocation overhead and cold starts. The right choice depends on traffic pattern, payload size, and latency requirements — not a blanket cost rule.

code.label: Async vs real-time endpoint creation (SageMaker SDK)
lines: # Asynchronous endpoint (large payloads, can scale to 0)

q: Walk through the key stages of an end-to-end AI/ML pipeline and explain the role of each component.
a: A complete ML pipeline has six major stages. Data ingestion collects raw data from sources (databases, APIs, files) into a central store — on AWS this is typically S3 as the data lake. Data preparation cleans, deduplicates, handles missing values, and transforms raw data into a structured form; SageMaker Processing jobs run these workloads at scale using sklearn, Spark, or custom containers. Feature engineering creates model-relevant numeric representations from the prepared data; SageMaker Feature Store stores and serves features consistently across training and inference to prevent train-serve skew. Model training applies a learning algorithm to the feature dataset to produce a model artifact; SageMaker Training Jobs manage distributed training on GPU/CPU fleets. Model evaluation tests the trained model against a held-out test set, measuring metrics like AUC, RMSE, or F1; SageMaker Clarify can run bias and explainability reports during this step. Deployment serves the model for predictions via one of the four inference modes. Monitoring tracks data drift, concept drift, and model quality in production; SageMaker Model Monitor triggers retraining when thresholds are breached. Model Registry (part of SageMaker) provides versioning and approval gates across the pipeline.
fj: Feature engineering happens after model training when you realise which inputs the model needs.
fs: Feature engineering is done before training as part of data preparation. The model is trained on the engineered features. Doing it after training would require retraining from scratch. Proper separation between training and serving features is enforced via a Feature Store to prevent leakage.

code.label: SageMaker Pipeline skeleton (data prep → train → evaluate)

q: What are the four key advantages of generative AI that distinguish it from traditional predictive ML?
a: The AWS exam identifies four core advantages: adaptability, responsiveness, conversational capabilities, and the ability to generate content. Adaptability: foundation models trained on broad data can be adapted to new tasks with minimal labeled examples via prompt engineering, few-shot learning, or fine-tuning — traditional ML requires a full labeled dataset and retraining for every new task. Responsiveness: GenAI models can accept free-form natural language instructions and return contextually appropriate responses without predefined input schemas, making them far easier to integrate into dynamic workflows. Conversational capabilities: models like Claude or Amazon Titan Text maintain conversational context across turns, enabling multi-turn dialogue for customer service, tutoring, or complex question answering — traditional classifiers are stateless per-request. Ability to generate content: GenAI can produce net-new text, images, code, audio, and video that did not exist in the training set, unlocking use cases like creative writing, synthetic data generation, and automated code review — traditional ML only classifies, clusters, or predicts values from existing categories. On AWS, Amazon Bedrock provides access to multiple foundation models (Claude, Titan, Llama, Stable Diffusion) that exhibit all four advantages.
fj: GenAI is only useful for generating marketing copy; traditional ML handles all analytical and decision-making tasks better.
fs: GenAI excels at structured reasoning tasks too — code generation, SQL writing, data analysis with Code Interpreter, document summarisation — not just creative content. Its adaptability and conversational interface are now widely used for data engineering, analytics, and business process automation on AWS.

q: Name and explain the six pillars of responsible AI as defined in the AWS AI Practitioner exam guide.
a: AWS organises responsible AI around six pillars. Fairness means the model does not produce systematically biased outcomes that disadvantage groups based on protected attributes like gender, race, or age; SageMaker Clarify measures disparate impact metrics pre- and post-deployment. Explainability means stakeholders can understand why the model made a given decision; SageMaker Clarify's SHAP-based feature attribution and Model Cards provide the audit trail required in regulated industries. Privacy and Security means personal data used for training and inference is protected — encryption at rest and in transit, data minimisation, and access controls via IAM and KMS are all in scope. Robustness means the system performs reliably under noisy, adversarial, or out-of-distribution inputs without catastrophic failure; techniques include adversarial testing and input validation. Governance means formal policies, accountability structures, and controls are in place to manage AI risk throughout the lifecycle — AWS Audit Manager's Generative AI Best Practices framework helps automate evidence collection for these controls. Transparency means being open about how the AI system works, what data it was trained on, and the limitations of its outputs; SageMaker Model Cards and Bedrock's model provider disclosures support this pillar.
fj: Bias and fairness are the same pillar — fixing bias automatically guarantees fairness.
fs: Bias is a technical cause (skewed data, flawed labels), while fairness is an outcome metric (equitable treatment across groups). A model can have residual bias in its data but still be approximately fair if the bias does not disadvantage any group disproportionately — or conversely, bias in one direction can be corrected while introducing unfairness in another. They must be tracked independently.

q: An executive asks why your team's AI system flagged 18% of loan applications from one demographic group as high-risk vs 6% from another. How do you investigate and address this using AWS responsible AI tools?
a: This is a classic disparate impact bias problem. Investigation starts with SageMaker Clarify: you run a pre-training bias report to compute the Class Imbalance (CI) metric and the Difference in Positive Proportions in Labels (DPPL) metric to see if the training data itself is skewed. You also run a post-training bias report after training to compute Disparate Impact (DI — ideal value is 1.0) and Accuracy Difference across demographic groups; SageMaker Clarify integrates with Model Monitor so these metrics can be tracked in production. Next you use Clarify's SHAP explainability to identify which features are driving the high-risk predictions — if zip code or income are highly weighted and correlate with protected attributes, that is a proxy discrimination signal. Mitigation options include resampling/reweighting training data to balance outcomes, removing or transforming proxy features, applying fairness-aware algorithms (adversarial debiasing), or setting decision thresholds independently per group to equalise false-positive rates. All findings should be documented in a SageMaker Model Card with the intended use, risk rating, evaluation results, and fairness observations before the model goes to production. For the executive, you explain: the model reflects historical patterns in training data, and regulatory compliance (Equal Credit Opportunity Act) requires equitable outcomes — you will retrain with bias mitigations and monitor monthly.
fj: Disparate impact can be fixed by simply removing the protected attribute (demographic column) from the training data.
fs: Removing the protected attribute does not eliminate disparate impact because other features (zip code, income, credit history) can serve as proxies for the protected attribute. This is called redlining by proxy. Clarify's SHAP analysis is needed to surface these proxy features, and fairness-aware training techniques or threshold calibration are required to produce equitable outcomes.

code.label: SageMaker Clarify post-training bias check
lines: methods=['DI', 'AD', 'DPPL']   # Disparate Impact, Accuracy Diff, DPPL

q: What are the environmental and sustainability considerations when selecting a model for production, and what AWS guidance applies?
a: Training large foundation models has a substantial carbon footprint — training GPT-3 scale models has been estimated to emit over 500 tonnes of CO2 equivalent. When selecting models for production workloads, responsible AI practice includes evaluating these sustainability dimensions. Model size: smaller, distilled models (Titan Lite vs Titan Premier, Claude Haiku vs Claude Opus) consume significantly less compute and energy per inference; right-size the model to the task rather than defaulting to the largest available. Managed services over custom training: using pre-trained foundation models via Amazon Bedrock avoids the carbon cost of training from scratch. AWS operates data centres with a global average PUE of 1.15 (vs industry average ~1.25) and powers infrastructure with renewable energy, making cloud deployments more carbon-efficient than on-premises GPU clusters — AWS infrastructure can be up to 4.1x more energy-efficient. Region selection: AWS publishes a Customer Carbon Footprint Tool; choosing regions powered by higher proportions of renewable energy (e.g. us-east-1 has clean energy commitments) reduces the carbon intensity of your workloads. Batch over real-time: batch inference requires fewer always-on instances than real-time endpoints, reducing idle energy consumption. The AWS Well-Architected Sustainability Pillar formalises these considerations as part of the six pillars.
fj: Cloud AI is always environmentally worse than on-premises AI because data centres consume enormous power.
fs: AWS data centres are significantly more energy-efficient than average on-premises infrastructure due to hardware optimisation, economies of scale, and renewable energy investment. AWS research shows cloud workloads on AWS can reduce associated carbon footprint by up to 96% vs on-premises equivalents, though absolute impact depends on workload size and region grid carbon intensity.

q: Explain the difference between transparent/explainable models and opaque (black-box) models, with examples of each.
a: Transparent models expose their decision logic in a form humans can directly inspect and trace. Examples: linear regression (coefficients map directly to feature contributions), decision trees (each branch is a readable rule), logistic regression. Stakeholders can audit a transparent model's reasoning for any single prediction without external tooling. Explainable models are more complex internally (e.g. deep neural networks, gradient-boosted ensembles) but are made interpretable through post-hoc explanation techniques that approximate or attribute predictions. SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) values decompose any prediction into per-feature contributions; LIME generates local linear approximations around individual predictions. Opaque or black-box models (large neural networks, foundation models) produce outputs with no direct human-readable decision path. Opaque models often achieve higher accuracy on complex tasks but create compliance risks in regulated industries (credit, healthcare, hiring) where the right to explanation is legally mandated (e.g. GDPR Article 22, US ECOA). The AWS recommended posture: use transparent models where accuracy is acceptable and explainability is mandated, apply SageMaker Clarify's SHAP-based explainability to black-box models to approximate feature attribution, and document all findings in SageMaker Model Cards.
fj: Neural networks are opaque and can never be explained, so they should not be used in regulated industries.
fs: Post-hoc explainability tools like SHAP (integrated into SageMaker Clarify) can provide local and global feature attribution for neural networks, making their outputs interpretable even if the internal weights are not directly readable. Many regulated industries (finance, healthcare) deploy deep learning models alongside Clarify explanations to satisfy audit requirements.

q: Your organisation must demonstrate AI model explainability and transparency to auditors. Which AWS tools do you use and what does each provide?
a: Four AWS services address transparency and explainability. SageMaker Model Cards are structured documentation artifacts attached to a model version in the Model Registry; they capture intended use, risk rating, training data sources, evaluation metrics, fairness observations, and deployment constraints — they are the audit paper trail for the entire model lifecycle and can be exported to PDF for non-technical stakeholders. SageMaker Clarify provides quantitative explainability via SHAP values computed during training (global feature importance) and inference (per-prediction local explanation); it also runs pre- and post-training bias metrics so auditors see both accuracy and fairness numbers in one report. Amazon Bedrock Model Evaluations provides automated and human evaluation of foundation model responses using metrics like correctness, faithfulness (hallucination rate), and harmfulness; when using an LLM-as-judge mode, Bedrock returns a scored explanation for every evaluated prompt-response pair, making model behaviour reviewable without requiring model internals access. Open-source models (Llama 3, Falcon, Mistral) deployed on SageMaker JumpStart offer full weight and architecture access, which is the maximum transparency tier — regulators can inspect training data lineage, architecture choices, and fine-tuning procedures. Together these tools form a layered explainability stack: Model Cards for governance documentation, Clarify for quantitative attribution, Bedrock Evaluations for output-level quality assurance, and open models when source-code-level transparency is required.
fj: SageMaker Clarify only detects bias; it cannot explain why a model made a specific prediction.
fs: SageMaker Clarify provides both bias detection AND SHAP-based explainability. SHAP values computed by Clarify show per-feature contributions to individual predictions (local explainability) and overall feature importance across the dataset (global explainability), addressing both fairness and interpretability requirements.

code.label: SageMaker Clarify SHAP explainability job
lines: # Output: per-feature SHAP values written to S3
# Viewable in SageMaker Studio Explainability tab

q: Match each AWS governance service — AWS Config, Amazon Inspector, AWS Audit Manager, AWS Artifact, AWS CloudTrail, AWS Trusted Advisor — to its specific role in securing and governing an AI solution.
a: AWS CloudTrail records every API call made to AWS services — including calls to SageMaker, Bedrock, and S3 — with timestamps, caller identity, and source IP; it is the fundamental audit trail for who did what to your AI infrastructure. AWS Config continuously evaluates resource configurations against rules (e.g. 'SageMaker endpoints must have KMS encryption enabled', 'S3 buckets storing training data must block public access') and flags non-compliant resources in near real-time, enabling continuous compliance monitoring rather than point-in-time audits. Amazon Inspector automatically scans EC2 instances, Lambda functions, and container images for CVEs and network exposure, which is relevant for custom ML containers and SageMaker notebook instances. AWS Audit Manager automates the collection of evidence from Config, CloudTrail, and other sources to map against compliance frameworks; it includes a pre-built Generative AI Best Practices framework for Bedrock customers that collects evidence for responsible AI controls. AWS Artifact is a self-service portal providing access to AWS compliance reports (SOC 2, ISO 27001, PCI DSS, HIPAA) and AWS agreements — teams use it to download audit reports that demonstrate the AWS-managed infrastructure layer is compliant. AWS Trusted Advisor analyses your AWS account and flags security gaps, cost inefficiencies, and performance issues using automated checks, including checks for public S3 buckets and unrestricted security group rules that could expose training data or model endpoints.
fj: AWS Artifact is used to audit your own application code for security vulnerabilities.
fs: AWS Artifact provides pre-generated AWS compliance reports and agreements (the vendor's audit reports), not scans of your own code. For scanning your own code and infrastructure, use Amazon Inspector (container/instance CVE scanning) or AWS Security Hub for aggregated findings across services.

code.label: Query CloudTrail for Bedrock model invocations (boto3)
lines: # Outputs: InvokeModel, ListFoundationModels calls with caller identity
